content.title: Luminous Objects
content.text: Objects that emit their own light are called luminous objects. These objects produce light through various mechanisms such as combustion, electricity, or natural chemical processes.

content.title: Non-Luminous Objects
content.text: Objects that do not emit their own light are called non-luminous objects. These objects may reflect light from other sources, making them visible to us.
The Sun is the main source of natural light on Earth. Apart from the Sun, other natural sources of light include stars, lightning, natural fire, and certain animals like fireflies and glow-worms.

content.title: Examples of Luminous Objects
items: Natural sources: Sun, stars, lightning, fire, fireflies, glow-worms
Artificial sources: Electric bulbs, LED lamps, fluorescent tubes, candles, torches, and kerosene lamps

content.title: Examples of Non-Luminous Objects
items: The Moon (reflects sunlight)
Objects on Earth (table, chair, book, mirror, metal plate)
Planets (Venus, Mars, Earth — they reflect sunlight from the Sun)

content.title: Example 1: The Moon
content.text: The Moon is a non-luminous object. It does not emit its own light. It shines because it reflects the light emitted by the Sun that falls upon it. This is why the Moon appears bright at night despite not producing its own light.

content.title: Example 2: A Firefly
content.text: A firefly is a luminous object. It produces its own light through a process called bioluminescence. Fireflies use this light to communicate with each other, especially during mating season. This is a natural biological light source.

content.title: Historical Context of Artificial Light
content.text: Humans have progressed through several stages of artificial lighting: (1) Fire (earliest form, using animal fat, oil, and wax), (2) Gas lamps, (3) Electric bulbs, and (4) Modern LED lamps. LED (Light Emitting Diode) lamps consume much less power, are brighter, and last much longer than traditional incandescent bulbs.

content.title: LED Lamps and Environmental Sustainability
content.text: LED lamps are more energy-efficient and environment-friendly. They reduce electricity consumption and bills. At the end of their life, LED lamps must be appropriately disposed of or recycled, not thrown in garbage.

notes.section_title: 11.2 Does Light Travel in a Straight Line?

content.text: One of the fundamental properties of light is that it travels in straight lines. When light is not obstructed, it always moves in a straight path. This property can be observed through simple activities and experiments.

content.title: Matchbox Activity — Light Through Holes
procedure: Take three matchboxes and make a hole in the inner tray of each matchbox at exactly the same position.
Arrange the three matchboxes in a straight line.
Ensure all three holes are exactly at the same height and are in a line.
Place a torch light on one side of the matchboxes, with the lamp at the height of the holes.
Place a cardboard (screen) on the other side and look for a bright spot on it.
Move one of the matchboxes slightly to a side or up and down.
content.observation: When all three holes are in a straight line, a bright spot appears on the screen. When one matchbox is moved out of alignment, the bright spot disappears.
content.conclusion: This demonstrates that light travels in a straight line. If light were to travel in curved paths, it would still be visible on the screen even when the holes are not aligned.

content.title: Bent Pipe Activity — Viewing Objects Through a Tube
procedure: Take a long hollow pipe made of flexible material.
Align the straight pipe so you can see a candle flame through it.
Observe the candle flame through the straight pipe.
Bend the pipe and try to see the candle flame again.
content.observation: The candle flame is visible through the straight pipe but not visible through the bent pipe.
content.conclusion: Light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around corners along with a curved path. If light could curve, it would still be visible through the bent pipe.

content.title: Example: Laser Beam in Water
content.text: When a low-power laser beam is passed through a beaker filled with water (with a drop of milk added for visibility), the beam follows a straight path through the water. This confirms that light travels in a straight line in different transparent media.

content.title: Advanced Concept — Light Bending
content.text: While light generally travels in straight lines, it can bend under certain conditions (refraction when passing between different media, diffraction around very small obstacles). These phenomena are studied in higher grades.

notes.section_title: 11.3 Light Through Transparent, Translucent, and Opaque Materials

topics.topic_name: Classification of Materials Based on Light Transmission

content.title: Transparent Materials
content.text: Transparent materials are those through which light passes almost completely. Objects can be clearly seen through transparent materials because light is transmitted without much scattering or absorption.

content.title: Translucent Materials
content.text: Translucent materials are those through which light passes partially. Objects are not clearly visible through translucent materials because light is only partially transmitted; some light is scattered and some is absorbed.

content.title: Opaque Materials
content.text: Opaque materials are those through which light does not pass. Light is completely blocked or absorbed by these materials, and no light is transmitted through them.

content.title: Examples of Transparent Materials
items: Clear glass panes (of windows)
Distilled water
Colourless plastic sheets
Air

content.title: Examples of Translucent Materials
items: Tracing paper
Frosted glass
Thin cloth (like muslin)
Watery ink solution
Wet tissue paper

content.title: Examples of Opaque Materials
items: Cardboard
Metal plates (steel, aluminium)
Thick cloth
Wood
Paper
Concrete
Human body

content.title: Light Transmission Through Different Materials
procedure: Collect objects made of transparent, translucent, and opaque materials.
Set up a torch and a screen (or wall) in a dark room.
Place each object between the torch and the screen.
Observe and record whether light passes fully, partially, or not at all through each object.
content.observation: Transparent materials allow light to pass fully. Translucent materials allow light to pass partially. Opaque materials do not allow light to pass at all.
content.conclusion: Materials can be classified based on their ability to transmit light.

content.title: Example 1: Clear Glass vs Frosted Glass
content.text: Clear glass is transparent — you can see objects clearly through it because light passes almost completely. Frosted glass is translucent — you can see shapes and light but not clear details because only part of the light is transmitted through it.

content.title: Example 2: Different Types of Paper
content.text: Thin tissue paper is translucent and allows some light to pass through, creating a soft glow. Thick cardboard is opaque and blocks all light, creating a complete dark shadow.

content.title: Relationship to Shadow Formation
content.text: The ability of materials to transmit light affects the nature and darkness of shadows. Opaque objects form dark shadows, translucent objects form lighter shadows, and some transparent objects may form very faint shadows.

topics.topic_name: Definition and Mechanism of Shadow Formation

content.title: Shadow
content.text: A shadow is a dark patch or area formed on a surface when an opaque object blocks the path of light rays. It is the region where light from the source cannot reach because the object is in the way.

Since light travels in straight lines, when an opaque object is placed in the path of light, the light is completely blocked. The dark patch formed on the surface where light does not reach is the shadow of the object.

content.title: Requirements for Shadow Formation
items: Source of light (natural sources like the Sun or artificial sources like a torch or bulb)
An opaque object that blocks the light
A screen or surface on which the shadow is formed (wall, floor, ground, etc.)

content.title: Observing Shadows of Different Objects
procedure: Collect objects of different shapes and sizes.
Set up a torch and a screen in a dark room.
Place each object between the torch and the screen.
Move the object to different positions and observe changes in the shadow (see Table 11.2 in the chapter).
content.observation: The shape and size of the shadow change based on the position of the object relative to the light source and screen. Shadows are formed in all cases when the object blocks light.
content.conclusion: Shadows form only when there is light, an opaque object, and a screen. The properties of the shadow are determined by the relative positions of these three elements.

content.text: The shape, size, and sharpness of a shadow depend on several factors, primarily the positions of the light source, object, and screen relative to one another.

content.title: Effect of Object Position Relative to Screen
content.text: When an object is moved closer to the screen (keeping the torch and screen fixed), the shadow becomes smaller and more similar to the actual object shape. When the object is moved farther from the screen (closer to the torch), the shadow becomes larger and may be distorted.

content.title: Effect of Object Orientation
content.text: When an object is tilted or rotated, the shape of the shadow changes accordingly. The shadow reflects the outline of the object from the direction of the light source.

content.title: Colour of Shadow
content.text: The colour of the shadow does not change when the colour of the object is changed. All shadows appear dark regardless of the colour of the object creating the shadow. This is because a shadow is simply the absence of light, not a coloured object.

content.title: Example 1: Your Own Shadow
content.text: On a sunny day, you cast a shadow on the ground. When you stand upright directly under the Sun, your shadow is directly below you and very small. As the Sun moves (or as you move to a different position), your shadow moves and changes in size and direction.

content.title: Example 2: Shadow Puppetry
content.text: In traditional shadow puppetry (Charma Bahuli Natya in Maharashtra, Togalu Gombeyaata in Karnataka, etc.), flat cut-out figures are placed between a light source and a screen. By moving the puppets and light, puppeteers create life-like movements. This ancient art form demonstrates how shadows can communicate stories and messages.

content.title: Shadows Formed by Different Materials
content.text: Opaque objects form dark, sharp shadows. Translucent objects form lighter, softer shadows. Even some transparent objects can form very faint shadows because of their edges or slight absorption of light.

topics.topic_name: Definition and Basic Concept of Light Reflection

content.title: Reflection of Light
content.text: Reflection of light is the change in direction of light when it strikes a shiny or polished surface (like a mirror). The light bounces off the surface in a definite direction instead of scattering in all directions.

A mirror is a smooth, polished surface that reflects light in a regular manner. The smoothness of the surface is crucial for reflection — if the surface is rough, light scatters in many directions (diffuse reflection) rather than following a predictable path.

content.title: Redirecting Light Using a Mirror
procedure: Take a shiny flat steel plate or a plane mirror.
Take it outside in sunlight.
Let sunlight fall on the shiny surface.
Turn the mirror in different directions to redirect light onto surfaces not directly illuminated by the sun.
Tilt the mirror in different ways and observe the light spot on the wall.
content.observation: A spot of light appears on the wall, and this spot changes position as the mirror is tilted. Light always travels in straight lines and changes direction when it falls on the mirror.
content.conclusion: A shiny surface or mirror changes the direction of light that falls upon it. This is reflection of light.

content.title: Observing the Path of a Light Beam Using a Mirror
procedure: Set up a torch, comb (to create a thin beam), black paper, white paper, mirrors, and a plane mirror with stand.
Using black paper, close all openings of the comb except one to create a thin slit.
Shine the torch light through the slit onto white paper to create a visible beam.
Place a plane mirror in the path of the light beam.
content.observation: The path of the light beam changes (is reflected) after falling on the mirror. The beam follows the law of reflection.
content.conclusion: Plane mirrors reflect light in a predictable manner, changing the direction of the light beam.

content.title: Types of Reflection
content.text: There are two types of reflection: (1) Regular Reflection — occurs on smooth, polished surfaces like mirrors, where light reflects in a definite direction; (2) Diffuse Reflection — occurs on rough, unpolished surfaces, where light scatters in many directions. In this chapter, we focus on regular reflection using mirrors.

notes.section_title: 11.6 Images Formed in a Plane Mirror

topics.topic_name: Properties of Images Formed by Plane Mirrors

content.title: Image in a Mirror
content.text: An image is the appearance of an object as seen in a mirror. It is the reflection of the object formed by the mirror due to the reflection of light from the mirror's surface.

content.title: Observing Images of Objects in a Plane Mirror
procedure: Take a plane mirror and a pen or other object.
Place the pen in front of the mirror.
Observe what you see in the mirror.
Move the pen to different positions in front of the mirror.
Compare the sizes of the images at each position.
Observe if the image is upright at each position.
Try to obtain the image on a screen placed behind the mirror.
content.observation: The image appears behind the mirror. The image is of the same size as the object regardless of position. The image is always upright (erect). The image cannot be obtained on a screen.
content.conclusion: A plane mirror forms an image that is of the same size, erect, and virtual (cannot be captured on a screen).

content.title: Properties of Images Formed by a Plane Mirror
items: Same Size — The image is of the same size as the object.
Erect — The image is upright, not inverted like in a pinhole camera.
Virtual — The image cannot be obtained on a screen. It appears to be behind the mirror.
Laterally Inverted — The left side of the object appears as the right side in the image, and vice versa.
Distance — The image appears to be as far behind the mirror as the object is in front of it.

content.title: Observing the Position and Distance of Image in a Mirror
procedure: Stand in front of a plane mirror and look at your image.
Notice how far it appears to be from the mirror.
Stand close to the mirror and observe if the image also appears closer.
Stand at different distances from the mirror.
Notice the relationship between your distance from the mirror and the distance of your image.
content.observation: When you stand close to the mirror, the image also appears to be close to the mirror. When you stand far from the mirror, the image also appears to be far from the mirror. The image appears to be as far behind the mirror as you are in front of it.
content.conclusion: The distance of the image from the mirror equals the distance of the object from the mirror.

content.title: Lateral Inversion
content.text: Lateral inversion is the left-right reversal of an image seen in a plane mirror. What appears on the left side of the object appears on the right side of the image, and what appears on the right side of the object appears on the left side of the image.

When you raise your left arm in front of a mirror, your image appears to raise its right arm. This happens because the mirror reflects light from left to right, causing a left-right reversal of the image.

content.title: Example 1: Raising Arms
content.text: If you raise your left arm, your mirror image appears to raise its right arm. If you touch your right ear, your image appears to touch its left ear. This lateral inversion occurs because of the way light reflects from a plane mirror.

content.title: Example 2: Ambulance Writing
content.text: The word 'AMBULANCE' is written in reverse (laterally inverted) on the vehicle so that when viewed through the rear-view mirror of a vehicle ahead, it reads as 'AMBULANCE' correctly. This practical application is based on the lateral inversion property of plane mirrors.

content.title: Why Only Left-Right Reversal?
content.text: In a plane mirror, only left and right are reversed, not top and bottom. This is because the mirror reflects light in a plane (2D), causing a reversal along the mirror's normal axis but not perpendicular to it. The image is still upright, not inverted top-to-bottom.

topics.topic_name: Definition and Principle of Pinhole Camera

content.title: Pinhole Camera
content.text: A pinhole camera is a simple optical device in which light rays from an object pass through a tiny hole (a pinhole) and form an image on a screen inside the camera. It demonstrates the straight-line propagation of light.

A pinhole camera works on the principle that light travels in straight lines. When light from an object passes through the small hole, only direct rays pass through, creating a sharp image on the screen. The image is inverted (upside down) because light from the top of the object passes through the hole and hits the bottom of the screen, and vice versa.

content.title: Simple Pinhole Camera Using Cardboard
procedure: Take a piece of cardboard and make a small hole (pinhole) in the middle.
In a dimly lit room, position the cardboard at a short distance from a screen.
Place a lighted candle in front of the cardboard (between the candle and the cardboard should be the light source).
Observe the image formed on the screen.
content.observation: Light from the flame passes through the hole and forms an image on the screen. The image is upside down (inverted). The image is smaller than the actual object.
content.conclusion: A pinhole camera forms an inverted image of objects. This demonstrates that light travels in straight lines.

content.title: Constructing a Sliding Pinhole Camera
procedure: Take two boxes (one smaller, one larger, such that the smaller can slide into the larger).
Cut open one side of each box.
On the opposite face of the larger box, make a small hole in the middle.
On the opposite face of the smaller box, cut out a square of about 5–6 cm and cover it with tracing paper to form a screen.
Slide the smaller box inside the larger box with the tracing paper facing inside.
Point the camera at a distant object in bright sunlight.
Move the smaller box forward or backward until an image appears on the tracing paper.
Observe the image and its properties.
content.observation: The image formed is upside down (inverted). The image shows the colours of the objects. The image is smaller than the actual object.
content.conclusion: A pinhole camera creates an inverted image of distant objects on a screen.

content.title: Properties of Images Formed by a Pinhole Camera
items: Inverted — The image is upside down, with top and bottom reversed.
Real — The image can be obtained on a screen (unlike mirror images).
Smaller — The image is usually smaller than the actual object.
Coloured — The image shows the actual colours of the objects.
Sharp — The image is clear and sharp if the pinhole is small and the screen is properly positioned.

content.title: Comparison with Mirror Images
content.text: Pinhole camera images and mirror images differ significantly: (1) Pinhole camera produces an inverted image, while a mirror produces an erect image; (2) Pinhole camera images are real and can be captured on a screen, while mirror images are virtual and cannot be captured on a screen; (3) Pinhole camera images are smaller, while mirror images are the same size as the object.

content.title: Example: Viewing a Distant Tree
content.text: When a sliding pinhole camera is pointed at a distant tree in sunlight, an inverted image of the tree appears on the tracing paper screen. The top of the tree appears at the bottom of the image, and the bottom appears at the top. This inverted image is much smaller than the actual tree but shows all the colours and details clearly.

content.title: Periscope
content.text: A periscope is a simple optical instrument made by placing two plane mirrors in a Z-shaped box arrangement. It allows observation of objects that are not directly visible from the observer's position.

A periscope works on the principle of reflection of light by mirrors. Light from an object enters the periscope from the top mirror at a 45-degree angle, reflects down the tube, and hits the bottom mirror at a 45-degree angle, which then reflects the light horizontally into the observer's eye.

content.title: Uses of Periscope
items: Used in submarines to see above the water surface without exposing the submarine.
Used in military tanks and bunkers to observe surroundings safely.
Used by soldiers to look over barriers or walls.
Can be made as a simple toy to look ahead when standing behind taller friends.

content.title: How Periscope Works
content.text: The two mirrors in a periscope are placed at 45-degree angles to the vertical axis. Light from the object strikes the top mirror, reflects 90 degrees downward along the tube, strikes the bottom mirror, and reflects horizontally 90 degrees into the observer's eye. This double reflection allows the observer to see objects that would otherwise be blocked from view.

content.title: Image Properties in Periscope
content.text: The image seen through a periscope is similar to the image in a plane mirror — it is erect and of the same size as the object. The image is formed due to the reflection of light from the two mirrors.

content.title: Kaleidoscope
content.text: A kaleidoscope is a tube containing three or more small plane mirrors arranged to form a triangle (or polygon), and a collection of coloured objects (beads, bangles, etc.). Multiple reflections in the mirrors create beautiful symmetrical patterns.

A kaleidoscope is constructed by joining three rectangular plane mirror strips together in a triangular arrangement, fixing them inside a circular tube, and placing coloured transparent objects at one end covered with tracing paper.

content.title: Components of a Kaleidoscope
items: Three rectangular plane mirror strips joined triangularly
A circular tube (made of thick chart paper) to hold the mirrors
Coloured bangles, beads, or other small objects
A transparent plastic sheet at one end
Tracing paper covering the transparent sheet

content.title: How Kaleidoscope Creates Patterns
content.text: When you look through the open end of the kaleidoscope, you see multiple images of the coloured objects due to multiple reflections inside the three mirrors. Depending on the number of mirrors and their angles, you see several copies of the same pattern arranged symmetrically. For example, with 3 mirrors arranged at 60-degree angles, you may see six images (or a pattern that appears to be multiplied). Each turn of the kaleidoscope creates a different pattern because the objects shift positions.

content.title: Interesting Features of Kaleidoscope
items: Multiple images are formed due to reflections of reflections.
A different pattern is formed every time the kaleidoscope is turned.
The patterns are always symmetrical and aesthetically pleasing.
Can be used both indoors (with coloured objects) and outdoors (pointed at trees and objects) to see beautiful patterns.
Designers and artists often use kaleidoscopes to get ideas for new patterns and designs.

content.title: Example: Creating a Kaleidoscope
content.text: When you construct a simple kaleidoscope with three 60-degree mirrors and place coloured beads inside, looking through the open end shows a beautiful pattern with six-fold symmetry. Each time you shake or rotate the kaleidoscope, the beads fall into new positions, creating a unique pattern. Even pointing the kaleidoscope at a tree or building outside creates beautiful symmetrical patterns of the natural scenery.

content.title: Mathematical Basis of Kaleidoscope Patterns
content.text: The number of images formed in a kaleidoscope depends on the number of mirrors and the angle between them. For three mirrors at 60-degree angles, typically six main images are visible (counting the object itself and its reflections). The formula for the number of images is related to 360° divided by the angle between mirrors.

content.title: Key Definitions
items: Luminous objects: Objects that emit their own light (Sun, stars, bulbs, fireflies)
Non-luminous objects: Objects that do not emit their own light (Moon, table, mirror)
Transparent materials: Allow light to pass almost completely (clear glass, water)
Translucent materials: Allow light to pass partially (tracing paper, frosted glass)
Opaque materials: Do not allow light to pass (cardboard, metal, wood)
Shadow: Dark patch formed when light is blocked by an opaque object
Reflection: Change in direction of light when it strikes a mirror
Image: Appearance of an object as seen in a mirror
Pinhole camera: Device forming inverted images through a small hole

content.title: Fundamental Principles
items: Light travels in straight lines in a uniform medium.
Light is reflected by shiny, polished surfaces (mirrors).
Images in plane mirrors are erect, of same size, and laterally inverted.
Pinhole cameras produce inverted, real images on a screen.
Shadows require a light source, an opaque object, and a screen.
Multiple mirrors can be used to create practical devices (periscope, kaleidoscope).
